component.name: CW Trainer
component.summary: Learn Morse code (CW) the Koch way, with copy practice and a QSO sim

description.p: CW Trainer by Wisco Radio Labs — learn Morse code (CW) the way hams actually use it, from your first two characters to a full on-air contact. Fully offline. No account, no network, no ads.

ul.li: Koch-method lessons — every character at full speed from lesson one, with Farnsworth spacing; a new character unlocks at 90% accuracy.
An eight-rung copy ladder — single characters, two-character groups, random letter groups, common words, a wider vocabulary, ham words, callsigns, and full QSO phrases.

Sending practice with a built-in iambic paddle and straight-key decoder that shows exactly what your fist sends — including the HH "start over" prosign. Fourteen drill categories climb from common words up through DX callsigns, contest fragments, and split/pileup work. Key on screen, with the keyboard, or with your own paddle or straight key through a USB adapter (VBand-compatible — the [ and ] keys).

A QSO simulator with six kinds of contact — ragchew, POTA, SOTA, IOTA, working DX, and contest — each with its own roles, plus on-air break-in fills, honest signal reports, and a 5-second "get ready" countdown before the code starts.
International and DX operating — a bundled DXCC entity dataset drives realistic foreign callsigns, prefixes, and exchanges, with a DX walkthrough in the on-air guide.
Realistic band conditions — selectable receiver filtering (wide / CW 500 Hz / APF), QSB fading, and AGC.
Reference guides on CW lingo, on-air procedure, and the history of the code.

description.p: Made in the Driftless by Wisco Radio Labs (K9MTE). GPL-3.0 · open source · fully offline.

screenshot.caption: Work a simulated POTA or SOTA contact
Paddle and straight-key sending with a live decoder
Copy practice across an eight-rung ladder
Track your progress across sessions, and tune speed, Farnsworth, sidetone, and band conditions

description.p: International and DX operating, plus fairer sending grades — with a stack of fixes and refinements from edge testing folded in:

ul.li: Work the world — a bundled DXCC entity dataset brings real foreign prefixes and callsigns into the trainer, with two new QSO activities (Work DX and Contest) and a DX walkthrough in the on-air guide.
Five new sending drills at the hard end of the ladder: DX callsigns, DX exchanges, contest fragments, split and pileup, and abroad callsigns.
Two copy rungs from a proper word list — "Common words" is now the top 500 of a frequency-ranked list built from public-domain texts, and a new "Wider vocabulary" rung steps up to ranks 1001–5000.
Your QSO sending is now graded on the elements you actually convey, in any valid on-air form, rather than on matching one scripted wording.

The QSO copy step is reworked around what matters most — reading the other station. Your copy box is now the main input, and the break-in keys tuck behind a "Break in — ask for a repeat" control until you arm them. The prompt now reads "Your copy — what did you hear?", with a reminder to type everything you heard, because the whole transmission is graded.

Compact option menus throughout, and the Copy level ladder now uses the app's compact selector — one control instead of a stack of buttons, with every rung's guidance visible. The Learn lesson picker keeps its type-a-number jump, now tidied into a single stepper row.

The practice layout keeps the on-screen key reachable without scrolling, down to a small phone screen (375x667) on the Key tab and both QSO keying steps, and the Easy-mode "Sending" readout now scrolls to keep your newest characters in view.
A blank or empty send is no longer scored 100%.
Timing ratings — letter spacing, word spacing, and dit/dah weighting — no longer show "good" when there was nothing to measure.
Copy accuracy is now tracked separately for Easy, Normal, and Real Life conditions (older records saved without conditions are grouped as "conditions not recorded").
On QSO copy steps, the break-in keys (?, AGN, QRS) now work from the keyboard — in earlier versions those keys typed into the copy box instead.
STOP now cancels the "Get ready" countdown before playback.
When a send can't be scored, the notice now explains why in plain terms — set your callsign in Settings.
While break-in is armed on a QSO step, the transport controls step aside.
Each tab's orientation text (Copy, Key, QSO) now stays collapsed once you start, and is remembered the next time you open the app — Copy gains a hide control it never had.
The app footer now shows the version number.
Small wording tidy-ups around the QSO steps.
The support ("Coffee?") link now points to Buy Me a Coffee.

An arm64 build is now produced and tested natively on every release. It is not yet published to stable, pending validation on real ARM hardware; ARM users can install it from the .snap asset on the GitHub release page.

The Settings profile fields (callsign, name, QTH) now have real labels, and the QSO copy box, receiver-filter group, cut-numbers toggle, and Settings headings carry proper accessible names (WCAG 2.5.3).
A one-time update to your saved progress removes older timing chips that were shown but never actually measured. Your best and last scores are unaffected, and it happens only once.
When your QTH is blank or can't be read, the app now leaves the state out of your exchange instead of inventing a "CT" you never sent.
New optional QSO auto-advance — turn it on and a perfect (100%) over continues to the next step on its own, so a clean exchange flows hands-free without leaving the key.
A friendly reminder at the end of every contact that real QSOs vary — it's all about building your confidence to get on the air.
The support ("Coffee?") link now points to Venmo.
QSO sending now auto-grades when you finish your over (it previously didn't fire).
Smaller fixes and polish.
Your sending grades itself the moment you finish a word — no button to press.
The copy box auto-focuses when it's your turn to type.
Color-coded bar charts with a 90% mastery line across Learn, Key, and Copy.
New QSO tracking — your copy and your sending are scored each contact.
The splash now sends your own callsign in Morse once you've set it, at your saved speed.
Character and keying speeds now go up to 40 wpm.
Steadier settings and progress saving across updates.
A small "Coffee?" link in the header to support development.

description.p: Version 2.0 — a big step up in feedback and progress tracking:

ul.li: New Progress tab: cross-session history of your lessons, sending, and copy, with dates and trends.
Fist feedback on your sending — estimated speed, dit/dah weighting, and element, character, and word spacing.
Eight keying drill categories, from common words to callsigns.
QSO roles — activator, hunter, chaser, or call/answer CQ — with varied, realistic CQ calls.
Iambic Mode B for paddle operators (opt-in).
A roomier, responsive desktop layout.
Accessibility, onboarding, and many smaller fixes and polish.

description.p: Answer Koch-method lessons with the keyboard, not just tap/click.
